data.title: The "Trap" of Predicting Markets: Why You Always Lose When You Buy
data.description:
Author: Terry Lee; Translator: BitpushNews

Foreword

On platforms like Polymarket, most people (including myself in the past) typically price parlays by simply multiplying the probabilities of each event together.

For example: The probability of event A occurring is P(A) = 80% The probability of event B occurring is P(B) = 70% The probability of event C occurring is P(C) = 60% Therefore, the total probability of a parlay is 80% × 70% × 60% = 33.6%. (Note that parlays are a term used in betting and investing, often called "串关" or "过关" in Chinese. Definition: You combine bets on two or more independent events. Rules:) You only win the prize if you predict all the events you choose correctly. If even one is wrong, the entire bet is lost. Sounds simple, right? The problem isn't the math, but the hidden assumptions. This multiplication operation assumes that each event is independent. This means that the result of A has no effect on B. But in reality, this isn't the case. For example: A decision by the Federal Reserve at one meeting can significantly influence the next. A presidential candidate winning the Rust Belt states foreshadows his chances of winning Pennsylvania, thus affecting his overall chances of winning the presidential election. In reality, most events worthwhile for parlay betting are related. If you ignore this correlation, you are very likely to pay too high a price or miss out on profitable opportunities. This article will present a simple framework to teach you how to scientifically price parlays, much like how the traditional financial industry has priced multi-leg options for decades. Why do pricing errors occur? In my opinion, most prediction market tools focus on "execution" rather than "correlation analysis." Furthermore, this niche market is relatively immature. While parlay betting is common in sports betting, pricing mechanisms are not yet fully developed when dealing with specific social/economic events because the market is still in its early stages.

Case Study: Federal Reserve Interest Rate Decision

(Figure 1: The Fed tends to repeat actions; in 83% of cases, it will "maintain" the interest rate again after initially "maintaining" it.)

Using data from the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis (FRED) (from 1994 to early 2026), I constructed a transition matrix specifically to extract changes in the Fed's decisions between consecutive meetings.

The results are very clear: Maintain -> Maintain: 83.1% probability -> Rate Cut -> Rate Cut: 69.2% probability -> Rate Hike -> Rate Hike: 62.5% probability -> Rate Hike -> Rate Hike: 62.5% probability -> Clearly, the Federal Reserve operates with "consistency." As a forward-looking, data-dependent institution, they tend to repeat the same actions until a "regime shift" occurs. How strong is this "consistency"? To test this, I built a model to identify every consecutive "decision trend" in history (i.e., every consecutive cycle of maintaining, cutting, or raising interest rates). The results are as follows: Maintaining interest rates: 32 trends occurred, each lasting an average of 5.4 meetings. Cutting interest rates: 12 trends occurred, each lasting an average of 3.3 meetings. Next, I simulated the history of the Federal Reserve in 1000 "parallel universes." In these simulations, each meeting was independent (similar to a coin toss). Based on total historical data, the probability of maintaining rates was set at 66%, cutting rates at 15%, and raising rates at 19%, but there was no correlation between each decision.

(Figure 2: The actual consistency of the Fed's decisions is 2-3 times the probability of randomness)

Under the simulation assumption of "mutual independence", the trend of maintaining interest rates can only last for an average of 2.9 meetings, while rate cuts and rate hikes can only last for an average of 1.2 meetings.

Comparing actual historical data with random simulations: Maintaining rates: Actual 5.4 times vs. Random 2.9 times (1.9 times more) Rate cuts: Actual 3.3 times vs. Random 1.2 times (2.8 times more) Rate hikes: Actual 2.6 times vs. Random 1.2 times (2.1 times more) It's worth noting that the consistency of rate cuts is almost three times that of random probability. This is because when the Federal Reserve begins to cut rates, it's usually to address a continued economic downturn, a problem that cannot be solved in a single meeting. They cut rates, assess the data, and if the data remains bad, they are highly likely to cut rates again. Simple multiplication calculations of "coherence" completely ignore these correlations. Real-world coherence is 2-3 times stronger than independent random models. What happens after two consecutive meetings? Simply looking at the previous meeting is insufficient; pricing "three-way links" (combinations of three events) requires studying the conditional probabilities based on the results of the previous two meetings. The analysis can be divided into two parts: Continuing the original path. (Figure 3: After two identical actions, the third action almost always matches.) As can be clearly seen from Figure 3, when the Federal Reserve repeats the same action twice, the probability of continuing the action a third time is overwhelming: Two maintenance -> Third maintenance: 87% Two rate hikes -> Third rate hike: 84%

  • Two rate cuts -> Third rate cut: 68% (slightly weaker)

    • Also noteworthy is the 0% cell in the matrix: the Federal Reserve has never suddenly cut rates after two consecutive rate hikes, nor has it ever suddenly raised rates after two consecutive rate cuts. They always go through a "pause (maintenance)" phase first. Just realizing this can help you eliminate a series of invalid combinations that "naive models" consider valuable.

    Two rate cuts -> Third rate cut: 68% (slightly weaker)

    It is also worth noting the 0% cell in the matrix: the Federal Reserve has never suddenly cut rates after two consecutive rate hikes, nor has it ever suddenly raised rates after two consecutive rate cuts. They always go through a "pause (maintenance)" phase first. Simply realizing this can help you eliminate a series of invalid combinations that "naive models" consider valuable.

    Cutting interest rates -> Cutting interest rates: Probability is 75%. Once the Federal Reserve breaks the maintenance stance and begins the first cut, the "floodgates" are opened, and the probability of follow-up is extremely high.

  • Rate Cut -> Maintain -> Maintain: Probability 100%. In recent history, the Fed has never immediately resumed rate cuts after pausing them. A pause means a complete stop!

  • Maintain -> Rate Hike -> Maintain: Probability 79%. The first rate hike after a maintenance period is often tentative; they will pause to observe the impact.

  • Rate Hike -> Maintain -> Rate Hike/Maintain: 60% and 40% respectively. Unlike rate cuts, pauses in rate hikes have real uncertainty.

    • The asymmetry here is the core insight. The combined value of "Maintain -> Rate Cut -> Rate Cut" is far higher than the price calculated by simple multiplication.

    The combination of "interest rate cut -> maintain -> interest rate cut" has historically had almost zero value. The same outcome, simply ordered differently, can have vastly different real value. Independent multiplicative models fail to capture this. What does overall pricing mean? This is the overall situation. We shouldn't use blind average probabilities, but rather conditional probabilities observed historically. Taking "three consecutive interest rate maintenance periods (Hold-Hold-Hold)" as an example: Initial model: Using the total probability (maintainment probability 67%), the calculation is 67% × 67% × 67% = 30.1%. Modified model: Using conditional probability, the calculation is 67% (first time) × 83% (second time | first time) × 87% (third time | first two times) = 48.4%.

    (Figure 5: Combinations of operations in the same direction are systematically underestimated, while combinations involving direction switching are overestimated)

    Real-time Market Detection

    Taking Polymarket data as an example:

    （Figure 7: Example of cumulative profit and loss for two consecutive trades）

    （Figure 8: Example of cumulative profit and loss for three consecutive trades）

    From Since 1994, betting $100 on each undervalued combination could generate $169,000 in two consecutive trades and over $1 million in three consecutive trades. The sharp jump in the yield curve corresponds to the Federal Reserve's easing cycles in 2001, 2008, 2020, and 2024-2025. In these cycles, the same actions occurred repeatedly, while the initial model consistently underestimated this consistency. The "stepped" shape of the curve tells us that money was earned during cycles of sustained Fed action. However, a limitation is that there may not have been well-developed prediction markets to execute these trades in the 1990s and 2000s. Where else can this be applied besides the Fed? The Fed case is typical because the data is abundant and highly correlated. However, the same framework applies to any related events: Presidential primaries: If a candidate wins in one state, his odds of winning in states with similar demographics change. Cryptocurrencies and macro/growth stocks: Bitcoin's price action is correlated with macro risk appetite. The value of betting "Bitcoin is higher than X and Nasdaq is higher than Y" is higher than the product of their independent probabilities, as they share common drivers. The approach remains the same in any case: look at historical data, measure the real correlation between events, use better data instead of blind averages, and compare with market prices. Conclusion: Prediction markets are still in their infancy. Most retail participants are still using a rudimentary "simple multiplication, let fate decide" approach when pricing "commodities." This framework requires contextual knowledge, but ultimately boils down to one question: Does the outcome of the first event tell you anything about the next? If so, then naive contango pricing is wrong, and historical data will tell you exactly how wrong it is. The Federal Reserve's case study shows that this advantage is real and measurable. But this principle is universally applicable. Wherever related events are priced as independent events, undiscovered opportunities may exist. The only question is whether you can see them and act on them.

data.title: Is Polymarket rushing to issue tokens to boost revenue figures?
data.description:
Author: Frank, PANews

On February 18, 2026, Polymarket announced that starting that day, the platform would pilot a market order fee in the sports market. The first wave of events covered are NCAA basketball and Italian Serie A leagues, with plans to gradually expand to all sports events in the future.

Previously, Polymarket's weekly revenue had already exceeded $1.08 million based solely on transaction fees from the 15-minute price fluctuations in the cryptocurrency market. According to on-chain data, the sports market accounts for nearly 40% of the platform's total transaction activity. If converted to annual revenue, cryptocurrency market fees alone could contribute approximately $56 million in annualized revenue. Therefore, when the sports market, which accounts for a larger share, also begins to charge fees, Polymarket may become the biggest money-printing machine in the crypto space.

PANews conducted an in-depth analysis of Polymarket's fee mechanism, revenue model, competitor benchmarks, and token airdrop expectations.

PANews conducted an in-depth analysis of Polymarket's fee mechanism, revenue model, competitor benchmarks, and token airdrop expectations.

From "Zero Revenue" to Millions a Week: The $9 Billion Behemoth Is Eager to Make Money. For a long time, Polymarket operated with virtually no revenue, charging virtually no transaction fees for most of its markets. This free strategy brought it astonishing growth: its total trading volume in 2025 reached $21.5 billion, accounting for nearly half of the total global prediction market trading volume ($44 billion); and its single-month trading volume in January 2026 broke records, exceeding $12 billion. However, with its upcoming coin listing this year, the zero-revenue model clearly cannot match its valuation. In its most recent funding round, its valuation reached $9 billion, and in October 2025, the Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), the parent company of the New York Stock Exchange, invested a whopping $2 billion in Polymarket. According to PM Insights, as of January 19, 2026, Polymarket's implied valuation in the secondary market had climbed to $11.6 billion, an increase of nearly 29% from its previous funding round. Reports suggest that subsequent funding rounds could value the company at $12 billion to $15 billion. Such a high valuation requires corresponding revenue to support it. The turning point came in January 2026, with Polymarket clearly showing signs of urgency this year. In January, Polymarket officially introduced a "Taker Fee" to its 15-minute cryptocurrency price change market for high-frequency trading, with rates reaching up to 3%. The effect was immediate: by early February 2026, weekly fee revenue exceeded $1.08 million, with the 15-minute price change market alone contributing $787,000 in one week in January, accounting for 28.4% of the total platform's prediction market fee revenue ($2.7 million) during the same period. To date, Polymarket has generated over $4.7 million in fees, ranking among the top in revenue. The ingenious design behind the 0.45% fee: a rate model that goes beyond just making money. Polymarket's new fees in the sports market are based on a carefully designed dynamic rate model. According to Polymarket's official documentation and community analysis, sports market fees are only charged to market orders (Takers). Limit orders are not only free but also earn a 25% rebate on the Taker fee. Similar to the fee model in the crypto market, the fee rate is not a fixed value but fluctuates with the probability of the event: Simply put, the more uncertain the market, the higher the fee. The fee peaks at 0.44% when the probability is 50%, while it drops to only 0.13%-0.16% when the probability is 10% or 90%. By standard, the fee structure of the sports market is much lower than that of the crypto market. However, this does not affect the revenue potential of the sports market. According to data, the sports market currently accounts for 39% of Polymarket's total transaction activity, surpassing political (20%) and crypto (28%) categories. More importantly, according to previous analysis by PANews, the average transaction volume in the short-term sports market on Polymarket ($1.32 million) is 30 times that of the average transaction volume in the short-term crypto market ($44,000). This means that if the sports market were fully opened to fees, revenue would see a significant increase. Taking the 2026 Super Bowl as an example, Polymarket's total trading volume in Super Bowl-related markets reached approximately $795 million, covering multiple sub-markets such as game results, player performance, and halftime show predictions. The total weekly trading volume in the prediction market has previously exceeded $6.3 billion, driven by sporting events. Based on existing data, PANews has constructed three profit forecast scenarios (assuming an average effective fee rate of 0.25% in the sports market, considering probability distribution and free limited-price deals): Even with the most conservative estimate, Polymarket's annualized revenue after full charging will exceed $200 million, enough to place it among the highest-grossing protocols in the Web3 space. While surpassing Tether's government bond interest income or Ethereum's mainnet gas fees is unrealistic, Polymarket has the potential to contend for the title of "most profitable dApp" at the application layer. Especially considering its 85% user retention rate, far exceeding typical DeFi protocols, this high stickiness translates to higher-quality revenue. POLY tokens and airdrops: a multi-billion dollar "feast"? Polymarket's high valuation and massive user base make its token airdrop one of the most anticipated events of 2026. Polymarket's Chief Marketing Officer, Matthew Modabber, has explicitly stated, "There will be tokens, there will be airdrops." Market predictions suggest a 62%-70% probability that Polymarket will launch its token before December 31, 2026. Considering the pace of the restart of its US operations, TGE is likely to be completed by mid-2026. On February 4, 2026, its parent company, Blockratize Inc., applied for the trademarks "POLY" and "$POLY," which is also considered a significant milestone for TGE. According to general practices in the crypto industry, it typically takes 3-6 months from trademark registration to TGE. The airdrop size may exceed Hyperliquid's, signaling the end of the era of inflated supply. Referring to the recent airdrop ratios of top projects (Arbitrum, Jupiter, Hyperliquid), the community share is typically between 5% and 15% of the total supply. PANews has calculated this based on different valuation assumptions: If the total airdrop amount is $1.4 billion, assuming 500,000 eligible active addresses, the average airdrop value per account could reach approximately $2,800. However, according to the Pareto principle (80/20 rule), top users could earn hundreds of thousands or even millions of dollars, and ordinary retail investors need to manage their expectations reasonably. Of particular note is that Polymarket launched a 4% annualized holding rewards program alongside its fee-based system, distributed daily based on hourly snapshots. This mechanism reveals the project team's clear preference: the duration of fund retention is far more important than transaction frequency. Moats and Hidden Worries: What are the risks of this "money-printing machine"? Charging fees means users need to pay additional costs, so how can Polymarket sustain this? The platform boasts a clear triple moat: First, it possesses unparalleled liquidity depth in the prediction market sector, crucial for high-volume traders; second, compared to traditional gambling's 5%-10% commission and Kalshi's 1%-3.5%, its 0.45% peak fee rate still offers an overwhelming cost advantage; third, ICE's entry not only brings capital but also data distribution capabilities. ICE plans to integrate Polymarket's real-time prediction data with global institutional clients, creating a "second growth curve" beyond transaction fees. However, risks cannot be ignored: Short-term trading volume fluctuations: Polymarket's monthly trading volume peaked at $1.026 billion in November 2025 before falling to $543 million in December. Will fees exacerbate this trend? However, considering the positive effects of increased market depth and narrower spreads after the introduction of Maker Rebate, long-term trading volume is expected to rise. Competitive Landscape: Kalshi holds a first-mover advantage in the US compliant market (approximately $260 million in revenue by 2025), Hyperliquid is attempting to enter the prediction market arena through "Outcome Trading" (FDV approximately $16 billion), and Predict.fun attracts users with DeFi yields. Regulatory Uncertainty: Despite obtaining a No-Action Letter from the CFTC and acquiring the compliant exchange QCX, changes in the US regulatory environment remain a Damocles' sword hanging over the prediction market. Postscript From free to paid, from the crypto market to global sporting events, Polymarket is undergoing a meticulously planned business model upgrade. While it can generate millions weekly from the crypto market alone, the sports market—a behemoth accounting for nearly 40% of the platform's trading volume and boasting 30 times the liquidity of the crypto market—is only just beginning to charge fees. Polymarket's story offers a thought-provoking model: the true value of a platform may not lie in how much money it earns at any given moment, but in proving its confidence to "charge when it wants." When the pie is big enough and the moat is deep enough, opening the floodgates to fees is only a matter of time. And this "money-printing machine," already in preparation, only had its start button pressed on February 18th.

data.title: Golden Morning News | Former Circle BD Joins Hyperliquid, SOL Breaks $85
data.description:
Headline

▌Former Circle BD Head Joins Hyperliquid Labs, Will Be Responsible for Global Developer Integration and Business Expansion

Sterling, former BD head of Circle, announced on the X platform that he has joined Hyperliquid Labs and will be responsible for business expansion and partnership building. Sterling's initial focus will be on collaborating with developers globally to expand Hyperliquid's buildercode integration ecosystem.

▌SOL Breaks $85

Market data shows that SOL has broken through $85, currently trading at $85.01, with a 24-hour increase of 1.99%. Market volatility is high; please manage your risk accordingly.

... As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $69,585.00, with a 24-hour change of approximately +0.87%; ETH price is $2,062.07, with a 24-hour change of approximately +0.36%; BNB price is $630.65, with a 24-hour change of approximately... SOL price is $87.77, a 24-hour change of approximately +3.34%; DOGE price is $0.112389, a 24-hour change of approximately +16.36%; XRP price is $1.52, a 24-hour change of approximately +7.80%; TRX price is 0.283463. The US dollar price fluctuated by approximately +0.50% in the past 24 hours; the WLFI price was approximately $0.1045, with a 24-hour fluctuation of approximately +1.44%; and the HYPE price was approximately $31.95, with a 24-hour fluctuation of approximately +0.15%.

Policy

▌CFTC Chairman: Federal Has "Exclusive Jurisdiction" Over Event Contracts, Will Sue State Governments Obstructing Regulation

The U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) Chairman Mike Selig stated that he has instructed the agency to submit an amicus brief to the court, clarifying that U.S. event contracts fall under federal regulatory jurisdiction, not state jurisdiction, and warned state governments challenging its authority to "see them in court." Selig pointed out that the CFTC has regulated related derivatives markets for over 20 years. Event contracts allow the public to hedge commercial risks (such as temperature changes and energy price fluctuations) while also acting as a check on news media and information dissemination. Currently, several states, including Nevada, have taken action against related platforms for alleged violations of sports betting laws. Previously, a federal judge in Nevada ruled that related contracts were not within the CFTC's scope of business, but that ruling is still under appeal.

Despite past legal conflicts with platforms like Kalshi, the CFTC has adjusted its stance on prediction market regulation following the Donald Trump administration's restructuring of the regulatory body. Selig stated that the CFTC will move forward with new rulemaking to interpret the Commodity Exchange Act "reasonably and consistently," promoting responsible innovation while adhering to the legislative intent of Congress. In response, Utah Governor Spencer Cox stated on social media that prediction markets are essentially "pure gambling" and indicated he would use all his powers to defend himself in court.

▌Stripe's Bridge Receives Preliminary Approval in the US to Establish a National Trust Bank, Allowing It to Issue Stablecoins

The US regulatory agency OCC has granted preliminary approval to Bridge, Stripe's stablecoin infrastructure company, to establish a national trust bank. This license will allow Bridge to issue stablecoins, custody digital assets, and manage reserves under federal regulation. Bridge stated that this move provides a clear federal framework for support for businesses, fintech companies, and crypto businesses. Previously, stablecoin companies such as Circle, BitGo, and Ripple also received similar preliminary approvals. The final approval date has not yet been announced. According to official news, cross-border payment platform Payoneer announced that it will partner with stablecoin infrastructure provider Bridge to provide its nearly 2 million customers with stablecoin holding, sending, and receiving capabilities. Bridge is reportedly dedicated to enabling businesses to quickly, securely, and conveniently embed stablecoin payment processes into their existing products. This collaboration will help Payoneer users directly use stablecoins for cross-border payments and settlements within their familiar product experience.

▌Vitalik Buterin: Network-layer anonymity is a key missing link in privacy infrastructure

Ethereum co-founder Vitalik Buterin stated in an article on the X platform that network-layer anonymity is a crucial component that has long been missing in the current privacy technology system, and it is of key significance for improving on-chain and off-chain privacy primitives. Current privacy technologies cover on-chain privacy solutions and off-chain zero-knowledge applications, but network-layer anonymity capabilities still have a significant gap. Furthermore, in some application scenarios, bandwidth requirements are low but latency is extremely sensitive; therefore, achieving a balance between low latency and high anonymity will be of great value to the development of related technologies.

...

▌JPMorgan believes the disruption brought by artificial intelligence will drive mergers among smaller banks

JPMorgan analysts say that smaller U.S. banks that struggle to keep up with AI spending may be forced to merge to offset the impact on their revenues. A team led by Vivek Juneja points out that while most banks are actively investing in AI capabilities to defend against competitors, growing concerns about the technology's impact have triggered a recent sell-off in the sector. Analysts say banks must increase their investment to keep pace.

Cryptocurrency

▌eToro Q4 results beat expectations, net profit reaches $69 million, a record high

eToro shares rose 14% on Tuesday due to better-than-expected fourth-quarter results.

This Israeli trading platform saw its full-year 2025 revenue grow by 10% to $868 million, with a record net profit of $69 million. Although Q4 crypto revenue fell to $3.59 billion from $5.8 billion, growth in stock and commodity trading filled the gap. CEO Yoni Assia stated that some crypto users are shifting to more volatile commodities. Entering 2026, January crypto trading volume declined by 50% year-over-year, but a diversified asset portfolio helped cushion the impact of the market downturn. eToro currently offers over 100 crypto assets to US users and continues its transformation towards an on-chain financial system.

▌Syler: Bitcoin falling to any level won't have a substantial impact on us

Syler: Bitcoin falling to any level won't have a substantial impact on us. People don't understand that we buy Bitcoin primarily based on equity, not debt. In 2024 and 2025, we raised $55 billion through equity financing, while only $8 billion was raised through debt financing. According to Arkham monitoring data, the World Liberty Fi linked address withdrew over 313.31 million WLFI from Binance in the past 11 hours, worth approximately $33.76 million. Eric Trump: American Bitcoin's Bitcoin Reserves Exceed 6,000 Coins Eric Trump announced on the X platform that American Bitcoin, the Trump family's mining company, has surpassed 6,000 Bitcoins in reserves in less than six months since its Nasdaq listing. Silicon Valley Investment Giant Peter Thiel Exits Ethereum Treasury Company ETHZilla According to market news, Silicon Valley investment giant Peter Thiel has exited Ethereum treasury company ETHZilla. It is understood that Peter Thiel's investment team previously acquired a 7.5% stake in ETHZilla (formerly 180 Life Sciences) through its affiliated entities.

    Important Economic Developments

    ▌Fed's Daly: The Fed Has About 75 Basis Points of Room Before Reaching the Neutral Interest Rate

    Fed's Daly stated that the Fed has about 75 basis points of room before reaching the neutral interest rate; inflation needs to be reduced; apart from healthcare and education, most sectors of the US economy face unemployment, and it is necessary to ensure that the fragility of the labor market does not develop into substantial weakness.

    ▌The Probability of the Fed Maintaining Interest Rates in March is 92.1%

    According to CME's "FedWatch": the probability of the Fed cutting interest rates by 25 basis points by March is 7.9%, and the probability of maintaining interest rates is 92.1%.

    The probability of the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates by a cumulative 25 basis points by April is 23.6%, the probability of keeping rates unchanged is 75.0%, and the probability of a cumulative 50 basis point cut is 1.5%. The probability of a cumulative 25 basis point cut by June is 50.0%.

    Golden Encyclopedia

    ▌What are long and short positions?

    Long and short positions represent opposing strategies used by investors and traders to anticipate price movements of an asset under consideration. In the cryptocurrency space, going long and short still apply to concepts from traditional financial markets. To profit from a rise in cryptocurrency prices, going long means buying it and expecting its value to increase over time. In contrast, going short in the cryptocurrency market means selling a cryptocurrency that you don't actually own in anticipation of a price drop, then buying it back at a cheaper cost to close the position and profit from the price decline.

    Cryptocurrency traders and investors utilize these strategies to navigate the high volatility and speculative nature of digital assets and capitalize on opportunities in both bullish and bearish market conditions. In cryptocurrency trading, a long position begins by buying an asset in the hope that its price will rise, while a short position begins by disposing of an asset (often borrowed) in the hope that its price will fall. Closing a position means buying the asset at a lower price to gain profit, while exiting a long position means selling the asset at a higher price to lock in profits. Entry and exit points are crucial for the successful implementation of these strategies. Cryptocurrency long positions have the potential to generate substantial profits through price appreciation, but they also come with significant risks of market volatility and potential losses. Despite these risks, cryptocurrency long positions have the potential to generate significant returns. The opportunity to profit from price increases is the primary benefit. Short cryptocurrency positions can be rewarding by betting on price declines, but they also carry significant risks due to market volatility, unlimited potential losses, and unpredictable price increases. Shorting cryptocurrencies requires precise timing, meticulous risk management, and continuous market monitoring to successfully navigate inherent volatility and maximize potential gains while limiting losses.

data.title: Why doesn't Web3 have OpenClaw?
data.description:
Author:Biteye Core Contributor Amelia

On February 15, 2026, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman announced on the X platform that Peter Steinberger, founder of OpenClaw, has officially joined the OpenAI team to lead the development of next-generation personal AI agents. OpenClaw, an open-source project that swept the globe in just one month, will be transferred to the foundation and will remain open-source and independent.

Founder Peter Steinberger is a veteran Austrian developer who founded PSPDFKit in 2011 and successfully exited the business for approximately €100 million, possessing 13 years of entrepreneurial experience.

XHunt's global influence ranking is 559, and on February 2nd, a description of ClawFather was added to his profile. ...strong>

XHunt's global influence ranking is 559, and on February 2nd, a description of ClawFather was added to his profile.

XHunt's global influence ranking is 559, and on February 2nd, a description of ClawFather was added to his profile.

Looking Back at OpenClaw: It Wasn't "Planned," It Was "Grinded" Out

1.1 From Farm Boy to King of PDF

Peter Steinberger

1.5 Rejecting Meta and OpenAI Acquisition

Before joining OpenAI, Steinberger faced several options.

According to TechCrunch, Meta attempted to acquire OpenClaw, but Steinberger rejected it. OpenAI also made an acquisition offer, which was also rejected.

His final choice was to join OpenAI, but not sell OpenClaw.

This means: Peter Steinberger joins OpenAI. OpenClaw is transferred to an independent foundation and remains open source. OpenAI commits to sponsoring, but not controlling. He can continue to serve as an advisor to OpenClaw. This arrangement is extremely rare in Silicon Valley. Usually, when large companies want an open-source project, they acquire it directly—like Microsoft acquiring GitHub and IBM acquiring Red Hat. But Steinberger insists on maintaining OpenClaw's independence. Why OpenAI? 2.1 "I've already played the startup game" In explaining his choice, Steinberger said: "I can totally imagine OpenClaw becoming a huge company. But that doesn't excite me. I'm essentially a builder. I've played the game of building companies, invested 13 years of my life, and learned a lot." Behind this statement lies the sober understanding of someone who has experienced a complete startup cycle. He knows how to raise funds, how to scale, and how to exit. He knew how to turn a tech project into a company valued at hundreds of millions of dollars. But he also knew the cost—burnout, loss of life, becoming a "dumping ground." More importantly, he knew that building a large company and changing the world are two different things. PSPDFKit is a successful company, but its impact on the world is limited. It made PDFs better on iOS, but that's far from enough. OpenClaw, however, has the potential to change the way humans interact with AI. 2.2 Shared Vision Leads to Greater Success In his blog, Steinberger mentioned that the more he communicated with the OpenAI team, the more he discovered that "both sides share the same vision." What is this vision? It is to make AI truly serve humanity, to make technology simple enough for anyone to use, and to combine open-source spirit with cutting-edge research. Partnering with OpenAI is "the fastest way to bring intelligent agents to everyone." This is not an ideological choice, but a pragmatic one. OpenAI possesses the most advanced models, the most powerful computing power, and the broadest user base. If he truly wants to "make intelligent agents accessible to mothers," OpenAI is the most realistic path. OpenAI has promised to transfer OpenClaw to an independent foundation, to sponsor but not control it, and to allow him to continue dedicating his time to maintaining the project. These promises are more compelling to a true builder than any financial terms. Why Web3 Failed to Take Over OpenClaw

Why Web3 Failed to Take Over OpenClaw

3.1 Web3's "Original Sin": Token Prioritizes Product

Web3

There's an open secret in the industry: most projects' primary goal is to issue tokens, not to develop a product. This isn't criticism, it's a fact. In the Web3 fundraising logic, the token is central. You need to design a token economic model first, then raise funds, then develop the product, and finally exit through an exchange. This logic leads to a structural problem: the product exists to support the token price, not to solve user problems. Let's see how OpenClaw would evolve if it were a Web3 project: Hypothetical OpenClaw Web3 version: September 2025: Released a white paper, announcing its intention to create a "decentralized AI assistant". October 2025: Received $5 million in VC funding, valuing the company at $100 million. October 2025: Launched a testnet, airdropping tokens to early users. November 2025: Listed on an exchange as an alpha token, market makers pumped the price, causing a 10-fold increase, with FDV reaching $1 billion. December 2025: The community begins discussing "when to launch spot trading" January 2026: The team is accused of insider trading, and the coin price plummets February 2026: The project is abandoned, the token's market capitalization is less than $10 million, a drop of 99% This is not fiction, but the true trajectory of countless Web3 projects. In less than six months, the project went "to zero". 3.2 The Trap of the Attention Economy A unique phenomenon exists in the Web3 industry: attention is more important than the product. A project's success largely depends on its ability to attract the community's attention. And what's the best way to attract attention? Create FOMO (Fear of Missing Out). Airdrop expectations, listing rumors, KOL endorsements, price surges… all of these can attract a lot of attention in a short period of time. But this attention is speculative, not practical. Users care about "Can I make money?", not "Is this product useful?". OpenClaw's growth model is completely different. Its 150,000 GitHub stars come from: Developers genuinely finding it useful; word-of-mouth recommendations from the tech community; and its ability to solve real-world problems. There was no airdrop, no listing on exchanges, and no KOL endorsements.

3.3 The Paradox of "Decentralization"

One ​​of the core concepts of Web3 is "decentralization," but ironically, Web3 projects themselves are often highly centralized.

One ​​of the core concepts of Web3 is "decentralization," but ironically, Web3 projects themselves are often highly centralized.

3.4 Fundamental Differences in Community Culture

Let's compare the community culture of OpenClaw with that of a typical Web3 project:

OpenClaw Discord:

  • Discussing how to deploy to Raspberry Pi

  • Sharing custom skills

  • Conclusion: The Second Half of Web3, It's Time to Learn from "ClawFather"

    Peter Steinberg's story reflects the divergence between two technological spirits:

    On one hand is the builder's spirit: I want to change the world, so I want to make something useful and solve real problems.

    On the one hand is the builder's spirit: I want to change the world, so I want to make something useful and solve real problems.

    On the other hand, there's the spirit of speculators: I don't care what value the project provides; I only care about issuing a token, exiting the investment, and making quick money. OpenClaw wasn't born in Web3, not because Web3 technology was inadequate, but because Web3 focused too much on listing on exchanges, pyramid schemes, pump-and-dump schemes, and exploiting investors. True builders would choose to stay away, just like Steinberg wrote in the server rules: "No talking about finance or crypto." This is perhaps the most incisive criticism and the most sincere advice for Web3.

data.title: From real estate to the internet, where is the secret to wealth in the next decade?
data.description:
Author: Sleepy.txt

Every generation has its own wealth code.

In the 1970s, when the spring thunder of reform and opening up had just sounded, the wealth code was written in the factory buildings of township enterprises and on the construction blueprints of Shekou, Shenzhen. Daring to go into business and buying real estate in prime locations was the most certain thing in that era.

In the 1980s and 1990s, when the first email was sent from China and when Yinghaiwei's time tunnel opened, the wealth code was written in the ".com" suffix and in the sleepless lights of Zhongguancun. Buying shares of Tencent and Alibaba and jumping into the internet wave was the most exciting choice of that era.

So, when we arrive in 2026, and the post-00s and post-10s generations begin to take center stage, where will their wealth secrets lie? The answer may lie in the lifestyle of this generation. To know what will be most valuable ten years from now, look at these young people today—where have they truly channeled their passion and talent? This leads to our story today. This story concerns the changing wealth concepts of a generation and the answers to the business world in the next decade. And the protagonist of our story today is Roblox. It may look like a children's playground, but it is becoming a social university for this generation of young people to learn business, practice finance, and earn their first pot of gold. Let's forget about the "metaverse," a narrative that was once overhyped and is now considered dead by everyone, and look at the story unfolding on Roblox. Alex Hicks was only 13 when he first encountered Roblox. Like other kids, he played games and made friends there. But soon, he discovered that what attracted him most to the platform wasn't playing, but creating. A year later, at 14, he began experimenting with the tools provided by Roblox to create his first game. He's been doing this for ten years. During those ten years, he hasn't worked a single day at a game company, nor has he received any professional programming training. At Roblox, he learned how to design game mechanics, how to price virtual items, how to maintain user activity through updates, and even how to manage a development team. By 2020, at only 24 years old, he already owned an independent game studio called RedManta, with annual revenue exceeding $1 million. Hicks' story is not an isolated case. Before entering Duke University, 18-year-old Alex Balvanz and his friends created a cops-and-robbers game called *Jailbreak*. Within months of its release, the game earned him $300,000 for tuition and made him a millionaire two years later. Behind these stories lies Roblox becoming the most important financial education classroom for this generation of young people. Through direct business practice, it has helped tens of millions of teenagers understand for the first time what income, expenses, profit, and return on investment are. Here, a massive creator economy is rising at an unprecedented pace. According to Roblox's annual economic impact report released in September 2025, the platform paid out over $1 billion to creators in the year from March 2024 to March 2025, an increase of over 31% year-over-year. The top 1000 creators earn an average of around $1 million per year, with significant growth. Of course, any mature business ecosystem requires a large denominator. Data shows that over 99% of creators earn less than $1000 annually. This precisely constitutes a real, even somewhat brutal, competitive business environment. The first lesson children learn here might be the Pareto principle of market economics. For this generation, their first financial education didn't come from their parents or school, but from Roblox. The first money they earned there, the first business model they learned, and even their first business failure will be deeply imprinted on their minds. This raises a question that worries all traditional financial institutions: when this generation of young people grows up, when their financial education is completed in a virtual game world, what should we banks, still waiting in line in the real world, do? Banks are forced to enter the game. The managers of TD Bank in Canada have clearly also seen this unsettling future. In March 2025, one of North America's largest banks officially launched a financial education game called *Treat Island Tycoon* on Roblox. In this game, players take on the role of a young entrepreneur building their own ice cream empire on a virtual island. They need to earn money, decide how to spend it, and learn about saving and borrowing. The game is completely free and its purpose is purely educational. TD Bank's decision was based on a detailed survey, in which as many as 86% of parents believed that interactive games or animated videos were far more effective than traditional books in teaching financial knowledge. In an interview, Emily Ross, VP of TD Bank, frankly stated, "Virtual experiences are reshaping early childhood education. What we need to do is provide a safe and fun entry point for children to subtly acquire financial literacy, laying the foundation for their future financial success." To translate this PR statement: We must find this generation of children where they are, not wait for them to find us. Where users spend their time, that's where the gateway to wealth lies. When Roblox boasts over 150 million daily active users, spanning a wide age range from children to young adults, it's no longer just a gaming platform, but a battleground for the minds of the next generation of financial users. TD Bank's anxiety is a microcosm of the entire traditional financial industry. Moreover, their competitors are no longer just other banks. Around the time TD Bank launched its game, the American fintech company Chime partnered with the beauty brand e.l.f. Beauty to launch another financial education game on Roblox. Even a beauty brand is vying for a foothold in financial education, which means the boundaries of financial services are being completely broken down, and the starting point of the competition has shifted to 10-year-old children. Traditional banks once believed their moats were physical branches, licenses, and substantial capital. But now they've discovered that their true moat may simply be the user's mindset. And the user's mindset is shaped during adolescence. When a child becomes accustomed to managing their assets, making transactions, and investing in the virtual world, how much demand will they have for physical banks when they grow up? When his first income comes from selling a virtual item he designed, instead of the New Year's money from his parents, how will his definition of assets change? This touches upon a deeper question: when the setting for financial literacy shifts from bank lobbies to the gaming world, we will be facing a group of digital natives with completely different concepts of wealth. How will they reshape the future financial world? When Virtual Assets Become "For the First Time" To understand the impending changes, we must first clarify a core difference: The relationship between this generation of young people and their parents regarding virtual assets has undergone a fundamental qualitative shift. Many might say that trading game items is nothing new, and it wasn't invented by Roblox. Indeed, those born in the 80s and 90s traded gold in World of Warcraft and bought and sold equipment in Fantasy Westward Journey. But the virtual asset trading of that time was fundamentally different from what we see today. During the heyday of World of Warcraft, Blizzard officially prohibited any form of cash transaction and ruthlessly banned accounts belonging to gold farming studios; players would even spontaneously hunt them down. Virtual asset trading was an underground black market suppressed by the official authorities. However, on Roblox, it was a paved road paved by the official authorities. You create content, players consume Robux to purchase it, and then you exchange this virtual currency for real US dollars through DevEx (the developer redemption program). This is the platform's core and most encouraged business model. Roblox's CEO even proudly announced in earnings calls that they had paid out over $1 billion to creators that year. The definition of assets among gamers has undergone tremendous changes with the development of the virtual world and games. In CS:GO, someone might offer $1.5 million for a rare weapon, and the weapon's owner might refuse to sell, deeming the offer too low. The entire CS:GO market capitalization has now reached $5.8 billion, resembling a massive independent economic entity. Players analyze skin price trends like they analyze stocks, even engaging in financial operations such as manipulation and short selling. A single game update once caused the market capitalization to evaporate by over $2 billion in a short period, a devastating event comparable to a small financial crisis. While those born in the 1970s were still teaching their children how to save money, those born in the 2000s had already learned how to trade in CS:GO. This difference is shaping two generations with drastically different financial worldviews. A 2025 survey showed that 51% of Generation Z in the United States own or have owned cryptocurrency, and a staggering 45% hoped to receive cryptocurrency as a Christmas gift. Meanwhile, the percentage of Gen Z users with traditional bank accounts has fallen below 50%. For them, the cash in their digital wallets, the skins in CS:GO, and the Robux in Roblox are essentially no different from the savings in their bank accounts. They are all digital, and can all be used for payments, transactions, and investments. The distinction between virtual and reality has long since blurred in their eyes. A report released by Roblox at the end of 2025 showed that as many as 70% of Gen Z users said that their virtual avatars' clothing on the platform directly influenced their shopping decisions and style preferences in the real world. The aesthetics of the virtual world are spilling over into the real world. Virtual assets are becoming real because the definition of "real" itself has been rewritten by this generation. The Invisible Inertia: Why do each generation's wealth ultimately end up heavily invested in the areas they were most familiar with in their youth? This is no coincidence; behind it are three invisible hands secretly orchestrating events. The first hand is cognitive lock-in. Legendary investor Peter Lynch famously proposed the investment principle: "Invest in what you know." The underlying logic of this principle is that people are naturally inclined to make decisions and investments in areas they are most familiar with, because familiarity brings a sense of security and reduces uncertainty. For those born in the 1970s, their tangible homes are the most certain assets. For those born in the 1980s, the internet products they use daily are the most credible investment targets. And for those born in the 2000s, they spend even more time in the virtual world than in the physical world. Virtual items that can be traded, flaunted, and generate social value are, for them, no less real and certain than a property in their parents' eyes. This kind of perception, rooted in their upbringing, is difficult to change once formed, and it will continue to guide their wealth flow for decades to come. The second factor is the intergenerational transfer of trust. For those born in the 1970s, trust was built on the nation and the land; a red property certificate was their ultimate source of security. For those born in the 1980s, trust began to shift towards business organizations and legal contracts; an option agreement bearing the company logo represented the right to share in the future value of a business organization. And for those born in the 2000s, the foundation of trust is shifting from authoritative institutions to virtual online consensus. They believe in code, algorithms, and the scarcity recognized by millions of players worldwide. A hash value recorded on the blockchain, or a skin with extremely low availability on CS:GO's global servers, carries a credibility in their eyes even greater than a prospectus for a financial product issued by a bank. The third force is the self-realization of network effects. When a generation collectively invests its attention, time, and money in an emerging field, a powerful network effect is created. The more people participate, the higher the value of that field; the higher the value, the more talent and capital are attracted, forming a positive cycle that ultimately self-realizes as the next era's wealth hotspot. The golden two decades of real estate and the entrepreneurial wave of the internet both followed this path. Today, hundreds of millions of young people are building new social networks, new economic systems, and new cultural identities in the virtual world. This force, formed by collective consensus, is laying the most solid foundation for the value of digital assets. Understanding these three principles is crucial to understanding why some US stock traders still believe Roblox is severely undervalued after the bursting of the metaverse bubble. In their eyes, Roblox is no longer just a simple gaming company, but an gateway to the future world of wealth, driven by cognitive lock-in, trust transfer, and network effects. The Mislabeled Company For a long time, people have been accustomed to measuring Roblox by the standards of game companies, comparing it to traditional gaming giants like Activision Blizzard and EA. However, the value of a financial infrastructure cannot be measured by the standards of a game company. Roblox's core business model is not about making and selling games, but about providing a complete, closed-loop economic system. In this system, it plays four key roles: First, it is the creator of the world. It provides the underlying physics engine, development tools, and servers, allowing creators to build their own virtual worlds at low cost, like building with Lego bricks. Second, it is the central bank. It issues and manages the only universal currency in this world, Robux. It determines the amount of Robux issued, the inflation rate, and most importantly, the exchange rate between Robux and the real-world currency (the US dollar). Third, it is the tax authority and payment gateway. For every transaction that occurs on the platform, whether a user buys virtual items or a developer withdraws Robux, Roblox takes a percentage as a fee. It processes hundreds of millions of micro-transactions every day, highly similar to Alipay or WeChat Pay. Fourth, it is the market regulator. It is responsible for reviewing all content on the platform, combating fraud and illegal activities, and maintaining the stability and fairness of the entire economic system. These four roles together constitute a typical platform economy. It does not directly produce goods (games), but profits by setting rules, providing services, and collecting taxes. This is exactly the same business logic as platforms like Alibaba and Amazon. From this perspective, Roblox's payment of over $1 billion to creators in 2025 has a completely different meaning. This is no longer "the cost of a game company," but rather "the disposable income of residents" in a vast economy. The economic activity generated by the entire platform is more like a country's GDP. However, why is such a vast economy still continuously losing money? This is precisely the root of the market's confusion about it and the core reason why it is underestimated. Roblox's losses are structural and deliberately chosen. Its revenue and cost structure is completely different from traditional companies. For every $1 spent, approximately 49% of the money is spent before Roblox can recognize it as revenue. Of this, 22% goes to app stores like Apple and Google as channel fees, and another 27% goes directly to creators. The remaining money is used to cover server, R&D, and management costs. This model is unacceptable to investors seeking short-term profits. However, it becomes easier to understand if compared to the early days of Alipay. Alipay also suffered huge losses for many years in its early years because it bore all the costs of infrastructure construction and user education, cultivating a generation's mobile payment habits through massive subsidies. When everyone gets used to paying with QR codes, and when it becomes an indispensable infrastructure of the entire commercial society, its trillion-dollar value will truly be realized. Of course, Roblox and Alipay are still fundamentally different. The former is rooted in entertainment scenarios, while the latter addresses the basic need for payment. However, they are strikingly similar in their strategic logic of exchanging losses for generational habits. The receding tide of the metaverse is actually a good thing for Roblox. It washes away the speculative bubble, preventing people from confusing it with empty concepts, thus allowing them to see its true value: a financial infrastructure rooted in the daily lives of hundreds of millions of young people, possessing a powerful network effect and a closed-loop economic system. The Answer to the Next Decade Financial education for an era is shifting from the physical world to the digital world. When a generation's financial enlightenment begins with business practices in a virtual world; when their first asset is a tradable game skin; when their trust in digital wallets surpasses their reliance on physical banks, a completely new economic paradigm has begun. How we understand platforms like Roblox today will determine how we understand the business transformation of the next decade. When a company's users are simultaneously its consumers, producers, promoters, and investors, are traditional valuation models still effective? When a company's core product is not a commodity, but a set of economic rules and a circulating currency, how should we define its boundaries? The answers to these questions cannot be found in today's financial statements. However, the hundreds of millions of young people creating, trading, and socializing in the virtual world are converging into a powerful force reshaping the future business landscape. Historical experience has repeatedly proven that understanding what young people define as assets is key to understanding the future flow of wealth. The wealth code of each generation is written in every new consensus reached by that generation.

data.title: Bitcoin and the New World Order
data.description:
Author: Arch; Source: X, @ArchLending; Translated by: Shaw & Jinse Finance

For years, Bitcoin holders have been accused of acting prematurely, being paranoid, or chasing a speculative fantasy. Just days ago, the world's most influential leader, at the Munich Security Conference, effectively endorsed this view.

Ray Dalio, in an article now widely circulated online, explained bluntly: The world order established after 1945 has officially collapsed.

The old rules no longer apply, and for those who have been holding Bitcoin and wondering when the world will see what they've seen…

That moment has arrived.

This moment has arrived.

Every monetary system has its expiration date. History tells us that monetary systems do not last forever, but evolve along with the geopolitical order that underpins them. The gold standard gave way to the Bretton Woods system; the Bretton Woods system gave way to the petrodollar system. Each transition was chaotic, controversial, and only becomes apparent in hindsight. We may now be in one such transition period.

Fiscal deficits are widening in major economies, and central banks are grappling with a situation where the tools they have relied on for decades (low interest rates, quantitative easing, forward guidance) are becoming less effective while their side effects are increasing.

Meanwhile, currency itself is being used as a geopolitical tool in ways unimaginable to the previous generation. The freezing of Russia's central bank reserves in 2022 was a watershed moment because it revealed structural problems. Any country's reserves, if held within another country's financial system, could be cut off overnight. This understanding has reached not only Moscow, but also Riyadh, New Delhi, and the finance ministries of all sovereign nations. Bitcoin was not born for the old world. People often discuss Bitcoin from a price perspective, but its deeper significance lies in structural factors. It is the first monetary asset in history that is not controlled by any single country, institution, or alliance. In the old order, characterized by relatively stable alliances, predictable monetary policies, and widespread trust in institutions, these characteristics were theoretically interesting but not urgent. In this new order, defined by fragmented alliances, competing economic blocs, and the active weaponization of financial infrastructure, these characteristics become extremely useful. Bitcoin is a means of combating systemic instability stemming from the rewriting of the rules of the game. What forward-thinking holders are considering: The emerging world order creates a set of conditions favorable to assets with specific characteristics: Cross-border portability; Unmanipulated scarcity; Settlement without intermediaries; Neutrality unaffected by the foreign policy of any single government. Gold has played these roles for millennia, so it's no surprise that central banks have significantly increased their gold reserves in recent years. However, gold has limitations in a digital, interconnected world because it's difficult to transfer quickly, verification is costly, and it's impossible to send it over the internet in seconds. Bitcoin addresses all these limitations while retaining the core attribute that gives gold its enduring value: the enforced credible scarcity through means other than political will. For investors focused on long-term investments, the question is shifting from "Will Bitcoin rise?" to "In a world with a volatile monetary system, where should I store value to ensure no single actor can destroy it?" The transition will not be comfortable, but that doesn't mean the road ahead will be smooth. Geopolitical transitions are inherently volatile. Short-term declines, regulatory uncertainty, and shifting narratives are characteristic of these periods. However, it's necessary to distinguish between asset volatility and systemic fragility. Bitcoin is highly volatile, but as world leaders now acknowledge, the traditional financial order is fragile. These are two completely different things. Those who can successfully navigate this period are likely to be those who understand this distinction and can tolerate short-term discomfort, because they see a longer-term trend. Looking to the future, old assumptions such as the dollar's hegemonic status being taken for granted, stable alliances, and monetary policy being apolitical are being re-examined globally, and the final outcome is still being written. But one thing is becoming increasingly clear: in a world that is gradually moving away from centralized trust and towards multipolar competition, assets that do not require trust in any single counterparty are forward-looking assets. The world order is changing. A question worth considering is whether your positioning reflects the world of the past or the world that is forming.

data.title: The Evolution of the Coin Listing Cycle: Yesterday's Wind Can't Make Today's Kite Fly
data.description:
Author: danny；Source: X，@agintender

If we compare the crypto industry to a set of teeth, then the path of coin listings over the years is like a process of "industry orthodontics." From the chaos of 2017 to the industrialized, streamlined distribution of tokens in 2025, each token allocation method in the market is essentially a correction of the industry's structural abnormalities and a challenge to the token structure.

In this process, the path taken by project teams to pursue top-tier liquidity has evolved from the early "voice-driven game" to the current "exorbitant dowry model" of offering huge sums of money; exchanges, in order to survive and acquire traffic and transaction fees, have also shifted from the early listing logic to the pricing logic. How exchanges, project teams, VCs, and traders destroy and love each other; slander and achieve each other. For you, a thousand times over. Introduction Teeth are a very "magical" organ in the human body. Why? Because teeth are the only organ in the human body that, after adulthood, allows us to deeply customize, move, and modify them through physical and biological means. This "plasticity" allows us to combat genetic misalignment and the wear and tear and discomfort caused by aging. We usually think of bones as hard and fixed, and that teeth, growing in the alveolar bone, should remain motionless. However, orthodontics (wearing braces) utilizes the characteristic of bone as a "dynamic and active tissue." When braces continuously apply a constant, light force to the teeth, the alveolar bone on the side of the tooth under pressure feels the stress, and the body sends out osteoclasts to absorb the bone in that area, making way for the tooth; while on the side where the tooth moves, the body sends out osteoblasts to fill the gap with new bone. The teeth simultaneously "break down" and "rebuild" bone, thus achieving slow movement within the skeleton. This is something that no other hard organ in the human body can do. After all, assuming you're not exceptionally gifted, you can't shorten your thigh bone or reposition your ribs by applying pressure, but you can with your teeth. The same applies to the rules and policies for listing on cryptocurrency exchanges. Part One: Listing on Cryptocurrencies = The Struggle and Transfer of Asset Pricing Power. This article divides the path of cryptocurrency listing into four stages: deciduous teeth – teething – malformation – orthodontics. The core issue running through these four stages is: who controls the pricing power of the asset. **Phase 1 (Community Pricing)** Pricing power was held by "market promoters" and grassroots communities. Traffic was king; whoever had the loudest voice was right. The result was that bad money drove out good, and the market was filled with noise. **Phase 2 (Exchange Pricing)** Exchanges regained pricing power through IEOs/Launchpads, acting as "gatekeepers" and "investment banks." The exchange's reputation became the core support for asset prices. The third stage (the collapse of VC pricing) saw VCs wield excessive pricing power in the primary market, making the secondary market unprofitable. Exchanges were forced to intervene, attempting to "rob the rich to help the poor" through coercive measures (Airdrop), but this was merely a painkiller, not a fundamental solution. The fourth stage (marketization/derivatives pricing) saw the funds for market speculation no longer in the spot market, thus transferring pricing power to more mature financial mechanisms. Through "contract trading" and pre-market trading, the market formed a fair price after sufficient competition, no longer relying on a single narrative or VC valuation reports. Part Two: The Historical Context, Logic, and Evolution of Crypto Listing Phase One: 2017-2018 "Baby Teeth Stage"—A Wilderness Era Where Voice Matters Core Path: Direct Listing, Community Voting During this period, the industry was in a "dentistless" state. The listing logic was heavily influenced by founder and community sovereignty; as long as a project could attract followers, it could gain entry. This was the "genesis" stage of Crypto. The industry was still in the era of pure trading platforms, with users primarily focused on the convenience, speed, and low cost of transactions. At the time, most mainstream exchanges were slow and unstable. New platforms built their reputation through "extreme simplicity," lacking complex learning systems or social features; their interfaces were designed entirely for experienced professional traders. **Reasons:** Customer Acquisition Anxiety: Startups needed to attract traffic from competitors quickly and cost-effectively. "Community voting" was not just about selecting coins, but also a battle for community belonging. **Compliance Vacuum:** Global regulators had not yet intervened, giving exchanges extremely high decision-making freedom. The logic was extremely simple: whoever had more fans guaranteed liquidity. **Method:** Represented by Binance's "monthly community voting for coin listings," users could vote by paying a small amount of tokens (e.g., 0.1 BNB). Winning projects (such as Zilliqa and Pundi X) gained top-tier traffic almost for free, but vote manipulation caused severe market misalignment, ultimately forcing them to abandon the program. Phase Two: 2019-2022 "Teething Period"—Ecosystem Building and Premium Issuance Core Path: IEO (Initial Public Offering), Launchpad, Launchpool, Direct Listing The industry began to adopt a corrective mechanism called "ecosystem." Exchanges were no longer just intermediaries, but "dentists" with in-depth due diligence capabilities. Background of the Era After the ICO bubble burst in 2017, fraud and technical vulnerabilities tarnished the industry's credibility. The market needed a safer and more reputable fundraising method. Meanwhile, the arrival of DeFi Summer (2020) made "liquidity mining" an industry consensus.

Reasons

Credit Repair: Exchanges, through Launchpad, introduced "bank-level" due diligence, acting as industry dentists to screen projects with legitimate teams and technology, upgrading the ICO model to the more secure IEO (Initial Exchange Offering).

Ecosystem Closed Loop: To solidify user stickiness, platforms forcibly empowered their own ecosystem tokens (such as BNB) through Launchpool, allowing users to obtain new tokens through "holding" rather than "buying," reducing participation risk.

2019-2020 (IPO Frenzy):

Launchpad (such as Bittorrent) introduced a pricing issuance model. Project teams not only had to pass technical reviews but also accept the exchange's pricing "suggestions" to ensure a certain "wealth effect" after listing.

2021-2022 (Lock-up Empowerment): Launchpool became mainstream, empowering platform tokens and marking a shift from "buying new tokens" to "mining new tokens." Users locked up platform tokens to receive new token distributions, forcibly binding project interests to the platform ecosystem. Third Stage: 2023-2024 "Abnormal Period"—The Game of High Valuation and Low Circulation and Mechanism Upgrades Core Path: HODLer Airdrop, Launchpool Background: Venture capital (VC) returned to the market on a large scale, giving rise to a large number of projects with valuations of hundreds of millions of US dollars but extremely low circulating supply (median only 12.3%). This structure leaves retail investors with virtually no profit potential in the secondary market, only continuous selling pressure from unlocked tokens. Simultaneously, hefty fines and CZ's imprisonment force a shift in focus from "wild growth" to "global compliance and stability."

Reasons

Pricing power conflict: VC-driven projects peak upon launch, depriving the market of price discovery. Exchanges, to protect their ecosystem, must use forceful measures to correct this, "returning profits to the people."

Compliance pressure: Starting in May 2024, rules explicitly favored small and medium-sized projects with high distribution ratios, requiring project teams to reduce the float portion, aiming to combat VC manipulation of pricing.

Corrective measures: The introduction of HODLer Airdrop and Megadrop for long-term holders forcibly distributed the "gift" directly to retail investors.

This is the most painful "periodontalgia" stage in the industry's orthodontic process. VCs have spawned a large number of projects that "peak immediately upon launch," with the median circulating supply of tokens falling to 12.3%.
