data.title: Dalio's lengthy article: The old order is dead; trade wars and capital wars will become the norm.
data.description: <div class="share___SVvtl"><div>Ray Dalio, founder of Bridgewater Associates, the world's largest hedge fund, released a major article on February 14th, officially declaring that the world has entered the sixth stage of the "Great Cycle"—a period without rules, rife with chaos, and where might makes right. Dalio's core argument is that the world order established after World War II in 1945 has completely collapsed, and conflicts between major powers will no longer be bound by international law, but will revert to primitive power struggles. He warns that this stage is typically accompanied by a mix of internal turmoil and external wars, until a new order is established through conflict. According to the latest developments cited by Dalio, at the Munich Security Conference held on February 14, 2026, major global leaders reached a rare consensus on the "end of the old order." German Chancellor Merz stated bluntly that "the world order that has lasted for decades no longer exists," and pointed out that freedom is no longer a given in this new era. French President Macron echoed this assessment, warning that Europe's old security architecture has failed and that preparations must be made for war. US Secretary of State Marco Rubio explicitly stated that the world has entered a "new geopolitical era." Dalio points out that, in this phase, international relations will follow the "law of the jungle." Unlike states that have police and judges, the international system lacks a powerful supranational institution to adjudicate disputes. When major powers clash, they do not seek legal solutions but rather resort to threats or war. This means that trade wars, technology wars, geopolitical wars, and capital wars will become the norm and may eventually escalate into military conflict. For capital markets, this marks the beginning of an extremely uncertain period. Dalio emphasizes that history shows that the risk of war is highest when two opposing major powers are militarily evenly matched and have irreconcilable existential differences. Investors must realize that in this phase, economic instruments will be fully weaponized, traditional hedging logic may fail, and the transfer of wealth and power will occur dramatically. In his article, Dalio details five main forms of conflict between nations: trade/economic warfare, technology warfare, geopolitical warfare, capital warfare, and military warfare. He points out that the first four types of warfare typically exist as intense competitive means, escalating long before the outbreak of hot war. Currently, the world is in a typical dilemma of great power rivalry—the "prisoner's dilemma." Neither side can be certain that the other will not attack, thus falling into a cycle of tit-for-tat escalation. Dalio's analysis suggests that while most competition aims for wealth and power, once this competition escalates into military conflict, conflicts across all dimensions are amplified. He warns in the article that the key to "winning" a war lies in achieving objectives without sacrificing core interests, but history is often filled with "foolish wars" caused by misjudgment and emotional decision-making. A Historical Mirror: Economic Warfare and Markets in the 1930s The article provides a reference for the current situation by providing a detailed review of pre-World War II history. Dalio points out that the Great Depression of the 1930s exacerbated wealth conflicts in various countries, leading to the rise of populism, authoritarianism, and nationalism. Whether it was the fascistization of Germany and Japan, or the increased trade protectionism in the United States and Britain, these were extreme reactions to the economic crisis. Dalio specifically mentions that economic and capital warfare had already begun in the decade leading up to the hot war. For example, the United States initiated a trade war in 1930 through the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act, and subsequently imposed a devastating oil embargo and asset freeze on Japan on the eve of World War II. This economic "strangulation" forced Japan to choose between compromise and war. From a market perspective, the German stock market initially rose due to military victories during the early stages of the war, but ultimately returned to zero following defeat; while the US stock market strengthened, stimulated by massive government spending and war-related needs. This demonstrates that market performance during wartime is highly dependent on a nation's chances of winning on the battlefield. Capital Warfare: Asset Freezes and Market Blockades In the current "sixth phase," the tools of capital warfare are being frequently mentioned and used. Dalio listed three classic capital warfare tactics: **Asset Freezing/Seizure:** Preventing an adversary from using or selling foreign assets they rely on, in extreme cases including unilateral debt default or direct asset confiscation. **Cutting Off Capital Market Access:** Prohibiting an adversary from accessing capital markets in their own country or a third country for financing. Embargoes and Blockades: Cutting off trade flows of critical goods (such as energy and technology). Dalio warns that, **because might makes right, the strong often exploit the weaknesses of their opponents for plunder.** In the event of escalating conflict, the security of traditional financial assets will face enormous challenges under such sanctions. The Logic of Wealth During Wartime Dalio concludes by analyzing the economic policies and wealth protection logic in a wartime environment. He points out that governments typically implement strict controls during wartime, including rationing, price controls, capital controls, and asset confiscation. To finance the war, governments issue large amounts of debt and monetize it, leading to currency devaluation. Therefore, Dalio advises investors to be extremely cautious about debt assets during this period. Historical experience shows that the best store of wealth during wartime is usually gold, because credit is often unacceptable or significantly devalued during wartime. He concluded that while the rise and fall of great powers is an inevitable part of history, the trauma of cyclical evolution can be managed if power is used wisely and productivity is maintained. However, in the present moment, the world must prepare for the impending upheaval and the painful adjustment to a new order. The following is the original text: Official Announcement: The World Order Has Collapsed Ray Dalio At the Munich Security Conference, the world order after 1945 was declared dead by most leaders, and the picture behind it is presented in the 2026 Security Report entitled "Deconstruction". More specifically, German Chancellor Friedrich Merz stated, "The world order that lasted for decades no longer exists," and we are in an era of "power politics." He explicitly stated that in this new era, freedom "is no longer taken for granted." French President Emmanuel Macron echoed Merz's assessment, stating that the old security architecture linking Europe to the previous world order no longer exists, and Europe must prepare for war. US Secretary of State Marco Rubio stated that we are in a "new geopolitical era" because the "old world" has disappeared. In my words, we are in the sixth phase of a great cycle, characterized by massive disorder stemming from a period without rules, where might makes right, and great power conflict. How this sixth phase operates is explained in detail in Chapter Six, "External Order and the Great Cycle of Disorder," of my book, *Principles: Coping with the Changing World Order*. Given the now almost universal consensus that the post-1945 world order has collapsed and we are entering a new one, I think it's worth your time to read this. Chapter Six: The Great Cycle of External Order and Disorder The relationships between people and the order that governs them, whether internal or external, operate in essentially the same way and are intertwined. In fact, not long ago, there was no distinction between internal and external order because there were no clearly defined and mutually recognized boundaries between states. Therefore, the six stages of the cycle from order to disorder within a state that I described in the previous chapter also apply to relations between states, with one important exception: international relations are more subject to naked power dynamics. This is because all governance systems require effective and agreed-upon 1) laws and legislative capacity, 2) law enforcement capacity (e.g., police), 3) adjudication methods (e.g., judges), and 4) clear, specific, and enforceable consequences appropriate to the offense (e.g., fines and imprisonment). These elements are either absent or less effective in guiding relations between states than in guiding domestic relations. While attempts have been made to make the external order more rule-abiding (e.g., through the League of Nations and the United Nations), they have generally failed because these organizations do not possess more wealth and power than the most powerful states. When individual states possess more power than the collective of states, the more powerful individual states dominate. This is because power is supreme, and equal wealth and power are rarely relinquished without struggle. When powerful nations clash, they don't have lawyers present their cases to judges. Instead, they threaten each other with either a deal or war. The international order follows the law of the jungle, not international law. There are five main types of conflict between states: trade/economic warfare, technology warfare, capital warfare, geopolitical warfare, and military warfare. Trade/economic warfare primarily refers to conflicts over tariffs, import/export restrictions, and other ways to economically harm competitors. Technology warfare concerns which technologies can be shared and which are considered national security protections. Geopolitical warfare refers to conflicts over territory and alliances; these conflicts are typically resolved through negotiation and explicit or implicit commitments, rather than combat. Capital warfare is a conflict imposed through financial instruments, such as sanctions, cutting off funding and credit by punishing institutions and governments that provide capital and credit, or restricting foreign access to domestic capital markets. Military warfare, on the other hand, involves actual firing and the deployment of troops. Most struggles between states fall into one or more of these categories (for example, cyber warfare has a place in all categories). These struggles concern wealth and power, and the ideologies associated with them. While most of these wars do not involve firing and killing, they are power struggles. In most cases, the first four types of warfare evolve over time into fierce competition between rival states, until military war begins. These struggles and wars, whether or not they involve firing and killing, are acts of one side exerting power over another. These can be comprehensive or restrained, depending on the importance of the issue and the relative strength of the adversary. But once military war begins, all four dimensions will be weaponized to the maximum extent. As discussed in previous chapters, all the factors driving internal and external cycles tend to improve or worsen simultaneously. When things go wrong, there are more things to argue about, leading to a stronger tendency towards conflict. This is human nature, and it's why we have large cycles that oscillate between good and bad times. Total war usually occurs when there are existential problems (so vital to the survival of the state that people are willing to fight and sacrifice for them) that cannot be resolved peacefully. The ensuing war will clearly define which side will prevail and hold hegemony in subsequent affairs. This clarity about who sets the rules then becomes the basis of the new international order. The following chart shows the cycles of internal and external peace and conflict in Europe since 1500, reflected in the number of deaths they caused. As you can see, there are three major cycles of conflict, each averaging about 150 years. While large civil wars and foreign wars last only a short time, they are often the culmination of long-term conflicts that led to their occurrence. Although the First and Second World Wars were each driven by classic cycles, they were also interconnected. As you can see, each cycle consists of a relatively long period of peace and prosperity (e.g., the Renaissance, the Enlightenment, and the Industrial Revolution), which sowed the seeds for terrible and violent foreign wars (e.g., the Thirty Years' War, the Napoleonic Wars, and the two World Wars). Both periods of prosperity (peace and fortune) and periods of decline (depression and war) affect the entire world. Not all nations prosper when the dominant power is prosperous, because some nations achieve this at the expense of others. As you continue reading, remember these two things about war: 1) it will not go as planned, and 2) it will be far worse than imagined. It is for these reasons that many subsequent principles concern methods for avoiding hot war. Nevertheless, hot wars will occur for both good and bad reasons. To be clear, while I believe most wars are tragic and fought for absurd reasons, some wars are worth fighting because the consequences of not fighting (e.g., loss of freedom) are unacceptable. As I explained in Chapter Two, after self-interest and survival, the pursuit of wealth and power is the most motivating factor for individuals, families, companies, nations, and peoples. This is because wealth equates to power—the ability to build military force, control trade, and influence other countries—and domestic and military strength are interdependent. Purchasing guns (military power) requires money, as does purchasing butter (domestic social spending needs). When a nation fails to provide enough of either, it becomes vulnerable to opposition both domestically and internationally. Through studying dynasties and European empires, I have learned that financial strength—the ability to outspend rivals—is one of the most important strengths a nation can possess. This is how the United States defeated the Soviet Union in the Cold War. Spend enough money in the right way, and you don't have to go to war. Long-term success depends on maintaining "guns" and "butter" without engaging in excessive behavior that leads to your own decline. In other words, a nation must be financially strong enough to provide its people with a good standard of living and protection from external enemies. Truly successful nations can do this for 200 to 300 years. No nation can do it forever. Conflict arises when a dominant power begins to weaken, or a rising power begins to approach it in strength—or both. The greatest risk of military war lies in both sides possessing: 1) roughly equal military strength, and 2) irreconcilable and existential differences. The choice faced by opposing nations—to fight or to retreat—is extremely difficult. Both are costly—fighting costs lives and money, while retreating costs status because it demonstrates weakness and leads to a loss of support. When two competing entities each possess the ability to destroy the other, both must have an extremely high level of trust, certain that they will not suffer unacceptable harm or killing from the other. However, situations where the prisoner's dilemma is handled well are extremely rare. While there are no rules in international relations other than those imposed on themselves by the most powerful, certain approaches do indeed produce better results than others. Specifically, those approaches more likely to produce win-win outcomes are superior to those that lead to lose-lose outcomes. Therefore, the crucial principle is: to achieve more win-win results, one must consider the most important matters for both oneself and the other party in negotiations and know how to exchange them. Skillful cooperation that creates win-win relationships, increasing wealth and power while distributing them well, is far more rewarding and less painful than wars that result in one side conquering the other. The key to doing this is seeing things through the eyes of your opponent and clearly identifying and communicating your red lines (i.e., what cannot be compromised). Victory means gaining the most important things without losing the most important things; therefore, wars that cost far more in life and money than they gain are foolish. But "foolish" wars still occur, for reasons I will explain later. People are all too easily drawn into foolish wars for the following reasons: a) the prisoner's dilemma, b) the tit-for-tat escalation process, c) the weakening side believing that the cost of retreat is too high, and d) misunderstandings that arise when quick decisions are required. Rival powers often find themselves in a prisoner's dilemma; they need ways to assure the other that they will not attempt to kill them, lest the other strike first. A tit-for-tat escalation is dangerous because it requires each side to either escalate or lose what the opponent gained in the previous move; it's like a "game of chicken"—pushing too hard will result in a head-on collision. Untruthful and emotionally charged rhetoric increases the risk of foolish wars, so leaders are better off being truthful and thoughtful in explaining situations and how to respond (this is especially important in democracies, where public opinion is paramount). The worst-case scenario is when leaders are untruthful and emotional when dealing with their people, and even worse, when they take over the media. In general, the tendency to shift between win-win and lose-lose relationships occurs cyclically. People and empires are more likely to cooperate in good times and fight in bad times. When an existing great power is declining relative to a rising great power, it naturally tends to maintain the status quo or existing rules, while the rising great power seeks to change them to conform to the ever-changing realities on the ground. While I don't know the part about love in the saying "In love and in war, everything is fair," I do know the part about war is true. For example, in the American Revolutionary War, when the British lined up to fight and American revolutionaries fired at them from behind trees, the British thought it was unfair and complained. The revolutionaries won, they thought the British were foolish, and the cause of independence and freedom proved that changing the rules of war was justified. That's how it is. This leads to my final principle: Have power, respect power, and use power wisely. Having power is good because power always prevails over agreements, rules, and laws. When things come to a head, those who have the power to enforce their own interpretation of the rules and laws, or the power to overturn them, get what they want. Respect for power is important because fighting a war you can lose is unwise; it is preferable to negotiate the best possible solution (unless you want to be a martyr, which is usually for foolish self-interest rather than wise strategic reasons). Using power wisely is also important. Using power wisely doesn't necessarily mean forcing others to give you what you want—i.e., bullying them. It involves recognizing that generosity and trust are powerful factors in creating win-win relationships, which are far more beneficial than lose-lose ones. In other words, many times, using "hard power" is not the best approach, and using "soft power" is preferable. When considering how to use power wisely, deciding when to reach an agreement and when to go to war is also important. To do this, one party must envision how its power will change over time. It is preferable to use power to negotiate an agreement, implement an agreement, or wage war when one's power is at its peak. This means that if one's relative power is declining, it is advantageous to fight sooner; if one's power is rising, it is advantageous to fight later. If you're in a lose-lose situation, you must get rid of it somehow, ideally through separation, though war might also be an option. Handle your power wisely; generally, it's best not to flaunt it, as this can make others feel threatened and build their own threatening power, leading to escalation and endangering both sides. Power is usually best kept hidden, to be drawn when a fight breaks out. But sometimes, displaying power and threatening to use it is most effective in improving negotiating position and preventing conflict. Understanding what's most important and least important to the other side, especially what they're willing and unwilling to fight for, allows you to find a fair balance that both sides consider a solution to the dispute. While having power is generally desirable, not having power you don't need is also desirable. Maintaining power consumes resources, most importantly your time and money. Moreover, power comes with a heavy burden of responsibility. I am often surprised that those with less power can be far happier than those with more power. Now that we have covered the dynamics and principles driving cycles of external order and disorder (derived through the study of numerous cases), I would like to briefly review the case of World War II, as it provides a recent example of the typical dynamics of the transition from peace to war. While this is only one case, it clearly demonstrates how the convergence of three major cycles—the monetary and credit cycle, the internal order/disorder cycle, and the external order/disorder cycle—created the conditions for a catastrophic war and laid the foundation for a new world order. The stories of this period are fascinating in themselves, but they are particularly important because they offer lessons that help us think about the current situation and future possibilities. To help paint a picture of the 1930s, I will briefly review the key geopolitical events that led to the formal outbreak of war in Europe in 1939 and the attack on Pearl Harbor in 1941. Then, I will quickly glide over the war itself and the beginning of the new world order in 1945 when the United States was at the height of its power. The global depression following the Great Depression of 1929 led to massive internal conflicts over wealth in almost every country. This led them to more populist, authoritarian, nationalist, and militaristic leaders and policies. These shifts are either to the right or the left, varying in degree depending on national circumstances and the strength of their democratic or authoritarian traditions. In Germany, Japan, Italy, and Spain, extremely poor economic conditions and less-than-perfect democratic traditions led to extreme internal conflict, shifting towards right-wing populist/authoritarian leaders, much like the Soviet Union, which, similarly experiencing extreme conditions and lacking democratic experience, shifted towards left-wing populist/authoritarian leaders at different times. The United States and the United Kingdom, with stronger democratic traditions and less dire economic conditions, have become more populist and authoritarian than in the past, but far less so than other countries. <h3 style="text-align: left;"><strong mpa-font-=""><span leaf=""><span text="">Germany and Japan</span></span></strong></h3><p style="text-align: left;"><span leaf="" mpa-font-=""><span text="">Although Germany was burdened with huge reparations debts after World War I, by 1929, through the Young Plan (which provided considerable debt relief and required the withdrawal of foreign troops from Germany by 1930), it began to break free of its shackles. But the global depression severely impacted Germany, leading to nearly 25% unemployment, widespread bankruptcies, and widespread poverty. Typically, a struggle ensued between left-wing populists and right-wing populists (fascists). The leading populist/fascist, Adolf Hitler, exploited feelings of national humiliation, inciting nationalist fervor and viewing the Treaty of Versailles and its imposed powers as enemies. He formulated a 25-point nationalist program and garnered support around it. In response to internal struggles and the desire to restore order, Hitler was appointed Chancellor in January 1933. Two months later, the Nazi Party won the most support and seats in the Reichstag. Hitler refused to pay any further reparations, withdrew from the League of Nations, and imposed authoritarian control on Germany in 1934. Holding the dual positions of Chancellor and President, he became the supreme leader of the country. In democratic countries, there are always laws that allow leaders to seize special powers; Hitler seized all of them. He invoked Article 48 of the Weimar Constitution, terminating many civil rights, and forcibly passed the Enabling Act, allowing him to pass laws without the approval of the Reichstag and the President. He showed no mercy to any opposition—he censored or controlled newspapers and radio stations, established a secret police (Gestapo) to eliminate and suppress dissent, stripped Jews of their citizenship, confiscated Protestant church funds, and arrested church officials who opposed him. He proclaimed the superiority of the Aryan race and prohibited non-Aryans from holding government positions. Hitler employed similar authoritarian/fascist methods to rebuild the German economy, implementing massive fiscal and monetary stimulus programs. He privatized state-owned enterprises, encouraged corporate investment, and actively worked to improve the living standards of Aryan Germans. For example, he founded Volkswagen to make cars affordable and accessible and oversaw the construction of highways. He financed these massive increases in government spending by forcing banks to purchase government bonds. The resulting debt was repaid through corporate profits and the monetization of the debt by the central bank (Reichsbank). These fiscal policies largely and effectively achieved Hitler's goals. This is another example illustrating how borrowing money, increasing domestic debt and deficits, can be highly effective if invested in projects that improve productivity and generate sufficient cash flow to repay the debt. Even if it doesn't cover 100% of the debt servicing costs, it can be extremely cost-effective in achieving national economic goals. As for the economic effects of these policies, when Hitler came to power in 1933, the unemployment rate was 25%. By 1938, it was zero. In the five years after Hitler came to power, per capita income increased by 22%, and between 1934 and 1938, the real growth rate averaged over 8%. As shown in the graph below, between 1933 and 1938, the German stock market steadily rose by nearly 70%, until the outbreak of the hot war. In 1935, Hitler began building up the army, stipulating that Aryans must serve in the military. Germany's military spending grew far faster than any other country because the German economy needed more resources to power it, and it intended to use its military force to seize those resources. Like Germany, Japan was exceptionally hard hit by the Depression, which made it even more authoritarian. Japan was particularly vulnerable to the Depression because, as an island nation with insufficient natural resources, it relied on exports to generate revenue to import necessities. When Japanese exports fell by about 50% between 1929 and 1931, its economy was severely damaged. In 1931, Japan went bankrupt—forced to exhaust its gold reserves, abandon the gold standard, devalue its currency, and deplete its purchasing power. These dire conditions and the vast wealth gap led to a struggle between the left and right wings. By 1932, right-wing nationalism and militarism had surged on a massive scale, hoping to forcibly restore order and economic stability. Japan began its invasion of Manchuria in 1931 and expanded throughout Asia by seizing necessary natural resources (such as oil, iron, coal, and rubber) and human resources (i.e., slave labor) from other countries. Like Germany, Japan could be said to have found that acquiring necessary resources through military aggression was more cost-effective than relying on traditional trade and economic practices. A severe famine in parts of Japan in 1934 led to further political turmoil and strengthened right-wing, militaristic, nationalist, and expansionist movements. In the following years, Japan's top-down fascist-controlled economy grew increasingly powerful, establishing a military-industrial complex to protect its existing bases in East Asia and the north and to support its aggression against other countries. Similar to Germany, while most Japanese companies remained privately owned, their production was controlled by the government. To understand what fascism is, we can examine three major choices a nation typically faces when selecting its mode of governance. First, there's the decision-making method: a bottom-up democratic model or a top-down authoritarian model. Second, there's the ownership of production. Finally, there's the value orientation: individualism prioritizing personal well-being or collectivism prioritizing the overall well-being. Fascism's choices in these three dimensions are clear: it adheres to authoritarian decision-making, upholds capitalist ownership of the means of production, and emphasizes collective interests as the highest priority. Fascists believe that top-down authoritarian leadership—that is, government directing production in private companies, subordinating individual satisfaction to national success—is the best way to make the nation and its people richer and stronger. In the United States, the debt problems following 1929 were devastating for American banks, restricting their lending worldwide and harming international borrowers. Meanwhile, the Depression caused weak demand, leading to a collapse in U.S. imports and sales to the U.S. from other countries. As incomes weakened and demand declined, more credit problems arose, creating a self-reinforcing downward spiral in the economy. The U.S. responded by turning to protectionism to safeguard jobs, raising tariffs through the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act of 1930, which further exacerbated the economic situation in other countries. Raising tariffs to protect domestic businesses and jobs during economic downturns is a common practice, but it leads to reduced efficiency because production doesn't take place where it's most efficient. Ultimately, tariffs can further weaken the global economy because tariff wars deprive the imposing countries of their exports. However, tariffs do benefit the entities they protect and can create political support for the leaders who impose them. The Soviet Union had not yet recovered from its devastating revolution and civil war of 1917–22, a war lost to Germany, a costly war against Poland, and the famine of 1921, and was plagued by political purges and economic hardship throughout the 1930s. Therefore, when conditions worsened in 1930 and tariffs were implemented, the countries' dire situation turned into a desperate predicament. Worse still, both the United States and the Soviet Union experienced droughts in the 1930s. Natural disasters (such as droughts, floods, and plagues) often lead to periods of severe economic hardship, and when combined with other adverse conditions, they can result in periods of intense conflict. Coupled with extreme government policies, millions died in the Soviet Union. Meanwhile, internal political struggles and fear of Nazi Germany led to the purge of hundreds of thousands who were accused of espionage and executed without trial. Deflationary depressions are debt crises triggered by debtors not having enough money to repay their debts. They inevitably lead to money printing, debt restructuring, and government spending programs that increase the supply of money and credit and devalue it. The only question is how long it takes for government officials to take such measures. Taking the United States as an example, it took three and a half years from the collapse of the Indian Reich in October 1929 to President Franklin D. Roosevelt's actions in March 1933. In his first 100 days in office, Roosevelt created several large-scale government spending programs, financed by substantial tax increases and massive budget deficits financed by debt monetized by the Federal Reserve. He implemented employment programs, unemployment insurance, Social Security support, and programs beneficial to labor and unions. Following his 1935 tax act (commonly known as "taxing the rich"), the top personal income tax rate rose to 75% (compared to a minimum of 25% in 1930). By 1941, the top personal income tax rate was 81%, and the top corporate tax rate was 31%, compared to 12% in 1930. Roosevelt also levied several other taxes. Despite all these tax increases and the resulting economic recovery, the budget deficit still rose from around 1% of GDP to about 4% due to such large increases in spending. From 1933 to the end of 1936, the stock market returned over 200%, and the economy grew at an astonishing average real growth rate of about 9%. In 1936, the Federal Reserve tightened monetary and credit policies to combat inflation and slow the overheated economy, causing the fragile U.S. economy to fall back into recession, which weakened other major economies as well, further exacerbating tensions within and between the country. Meanwhile, in Europe, the conflict between left-wing and right-wing populists in Spain erupted into the brutal Spanish Civil War. Franco, on the right, with Hitler's support, successfully purged the left-wing opposition in Spain. During periods of severe economic hardship and vast wealth inequality, revolutionary, large-scale redistribution of wealth often occurs. When carried out peacefully, this is achieved through substantial tax increases on the wealthy and increased money supply to devalue debtors' claims; when carried out violently, it is achieved through forced asset confiscation. In the United States and the United Kingdom, while a redistribution of wealth and political power occurred, capitalism and democracy were maintained. This was not the case in Germany, Japan, Italy, and Spain. Before a hot war begins, there is usually an economic war. Similarly, typically, there is a decade-long economic, technological, geopolitical, and capital war preceding the declaration of total war, during which the conflicting major powers deter each other and test the limits of their respective strengths. Although 1939 and 1941 are considered the official start of the war in Europe and the Pacific, the conflict had actually begun about a decade earlier. In addition to internal economically motivated conflicts and the resulting political shifts, all these nations faced escalating external economic conflicts as they vied for a larger share of a shrinking economic pie. With power, rather than law, dominating international relations, Germany and Japan became more expansionist and began increasingly testing Britain, the United States, and France in their competition for resources and territorial influence. Before proceeding with the description of hot war, I want to elaborate on the common tactics used when economic capital instruments are weaponized. These tactics were common in the past and are still used today. These include asset freezes or confiscations—preventing an adversary from using or selling the foreign assets upon which they depend for survival. These measures are wide-ranging, ranging from asset freezes targeting specific groups within a country, such as the current US sanctions against the Iranian Revolutionary Guard or the US asset freeze on Japan in the early stages of World War II, to more severe measures, such as unilaterally refusing to repay debts or even directly confiscating a country's assets. Another strategy is blocking access to capital markets, preventing a country from entering its own or another country's capital markets. For example, in 1887, Germany hindered Russia's military buildup by prohibiting the purchase of Russian securities and debt. Furthermore, embargoes and blockades are also common tactics, aiming to weaken a target country or prevent it from obtaining essential resources by hindering trade in goods or services within its own borders, and in some cases, by blocking trade with neutral third parties. Typical examples include the US oil embargo against Japan during World War II and the blocking of Japanese ships through the Panama Canal. Similarly, preventing a target country's exports from flowing to other countries can cut off its revenue streams, as seen in the French blockade of Britain during the Napoleonic Wars. The Hot War Begins. In November 1937, Hitler secretly met with his senior officials and announced his plans for German expansion to acquire resources and unify the Aryan race. He then put these plans into action, first annexing Austria and then seizing oil-rich regions then belonging to Czechoslovakia. Europe and the United States watched warily, unwilling to be drawn into another war so quickly after the devastation of World War I. As with all wars, the unknown far outweighs the known because a) rival powers will only go to war when their strength is roughly equal (otherwise, it would be foolish suicide for the significantly weaker party), and b) there are too many possible actions and reactions to predict. The only thing known at the start of a hot war is that it may be extremely painful, even devastating. Therefore, wise leaders typically only go to war when the other side has forced them into a situation where they must either fight or be defeated by retreat. For the Allies, that moment arrived on September 1, 1939, when Germany invaded Poland. Germany appeared unstoppable; it quickly occupied Denmark, Norway, the Netherlands, Belgium, Luxembourg, and France, and strengthened its alliances with Japan and Italy, both of which shared a common enemy and were ideologically aligned. By rapidly seizing territory (for example, oil-rich Romania), Hitler's army was able to preserve its existing oil resources and quickly acquire new ones. The desire for and acquisition of natural resources remained a major driving force for the Nazi war machine to push its campaigns into Russia and the Middle East. War with the Soviet Union was inevitable; the only question was when. Despite a non-aggression pact between Germany and the Soviet Union, Germany invaded the Soviet Union in June 1941, plunging Germany into a costly two-front war. In November 1940, Roosevelt won re-election after promising to keep the United States out of the war, even though the U.S. was already taking economic action to protect its interests, particularly in the Pacific region, by providing economic support to countries it sympathized with and imposing economic sanctions on those it did not. As early as 1940, Secretary of War Henry Stimson initiated economic sanctions against Japan, culminating in the Export Control Act of 1940. In mid-1940, the U.S. transferred its Pacific Fleet to Hawaii. In October, the U.S. strengthened the embargo, restricting the export of "all steel to all destinations except Britain and countries in the Western Hemisphere." This plan aimed to cut off Japan's resources to force its withdrawal from most of the occupied territories. In March 1941, Congress passed the Lend-Lease Act, allowing the United States to lend or lease war materials to countries it deemed "critical to U.S. defense," including Great Britain and the Soviet Union. Helping allies was geopolitically and economically advantageous to the United States, as it profited greatly from selling weapons, food, and other goods to these quasi-allies struggling to maintain production during the war. However, the motivation was not purely mercenary. Britain's funds (i.e., gold) were depleted, so the United States allowed them to defer payments until after the war (and in some cases, waive payments altogether). While not an explicit declaration of war, the Lend-Lease Act effectively ended America's neutrality. When a nation is weak, its adversaries will exploit its weaknesses for their own gain. France, the Netherlands, and Great Britain all had colonies in Asia. Overstretched with its warfronts in Europe, the United States was unable to defend its colonies against Japan. Beginning in September 1940, Japan invaded several Southeast Asian colonies, starting with French Indochina, incorporating its so-called "Southern Resource Region" into its "Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere." In 1941, Japan seized the oil reserves of the Dutch East Indies. Japan's territorial expansion posed a threat to America's own Pacific ambitions. In July and August 1941, Roosevelt responded by freezing all Japanese assets in the United States, closing the Panama Canal to Japanese ships, and imposing an embargo on oil and natural gas. This cut off three-quarters of Japan's trade and 80% of its oil supply. Japan estimated its oil reserves would run out within two years. This left Japan with a choice: either back down or attack the United States. On December 7th and 8th, 1941, Japan launched a coordinated attack on American military forces at Pearl Harbor and in the Philippines. This marked the official outbreak of the Pacific War and drew the United States into the war in Europe. While Japan lacked a widely accepted plan to win the war, the most optimistic Japanese leaders believed that the United States would be defeated by fighting on two fronts and because its individualistic/capitalist political system was inferior to Japan's and Germany's authoritarian/fascist systems and their command military-industrial complexes. They also believed that their greater willingness to endure suffering and sacrifice for the nation was a crucial factor in determining which side would win. In war, the ability to endure suffering is even more important than the ability to inflict it. Just as noteworthy are typical international economic warfare strategies, so too are typical wartime economic policies within a nation. These include government control over virtually everything as the state shifts its resources from profit to war—for example, the government decides: a) which goods are permitted to be produced, b) which goods can be bought and sold in what quantities (rationing), c) which goods can be imported or exported, d) prices, wages, and profits, e) access to its own financial assets, and f) the ability to transfer personal funds abroad. Because war is costly, governments typically: g) issue large amounts of monetized debt; h) rely on non-credit currencies such as gold for international transactions because their credit is unacceptable; i) govern more authoritarianally; j) impose various types of economic sanctions on enemies, including cutting off their access to capital markets; and k) encounter enemies imposing these sanctions on them. When the United States joined the European and Pacific Wars after Pearl Harbor, most countries implemented typical wartime economic policies, with leaders adopting more authoritarian methods that enjoyed widespread public support. Market fluctuations during hot wars are heavily influenced by government control and the performance of various countries in the war, as the probability of victory or defeat changes. Many countries generally closed their stock markets, trapping stock investors and preventing them from accessing their capital. I should also point out that during wartime, currency and credit were generally not universally accepted among non-Allied nations because there were reasons to worry about whether the currency still had value. As mentioned earlier, gold—or in some cases silver or physical exchange—was the hard currency during wartime. In such times, prices and capital flows are often controlled, making it difficult to determine the true value of many things. Because losing a war typically results in the complete loss of wealth and power, the performance of stock markets that remained open during wartime largely depended on the performance of nations in key battles, as these outcomes altered the probabilities of victory or defeat for both sides. For example, the German stock market outperformed other countries in the early stages of World War II, when Germany had occupied territory and established military superiority, but lagged behind after the Allied powers, such as the United States and Great Britain, turned the tide of the war. After the Battle of Midway in 1942, Allied stock markets rose almost continuously until the end of the war, while Axis stock markets remained flat or declined. As shown in the chart, the German and Japanese stock markets were closed at the end of the war and reopened approximately five years later, almost worthless, while the US stock market was extremely strong. Protecting wealth during wartime is difficult because normal economic activity is restricted, traditionally safe investments are not secure, capital mobility is limited, and high taxes are levied when people and nations are fighting for survival. Protecting the wealth of existing holders is not a priority compared to the need to redistribute wealth to where it is most needed. As for investment, all debt should be sold and gold bought, because wars are financed through borrowing and printing money, which devalues ​​debt and currency, and people have reason to be unwilling to accept credit. Every world power has its glorious period, thanks to its unique circumstances and the nature of its character and culture (for example, its strong work ethic, intelligence, discipline, education, and other fundamental elements), but they all eventually decline. Some countries decline more smoothly and less traumatically than others, but they will still decline eventually. Traumatic declines can lead to the worst periods in history, when the great wars over wealth and power prove extremely costly in terms of both economy and human lives. Nevertheless, if a nation in its prosperous phase maintains productivity, income exceeding expenditure, ensures its institutions function well for the majority of its population, and manages to establish and sustain mutually beneficial relationships with its most important competitors, then this cycle need not unfold in this way. Many empires and dynasties lasted for centuries, and the United States, at 245 years old, has proven itself to be one of the longest-lived nations.</span></span></p></div></div>
